column4: Sliney US4728448A Table II preferred PS200 composition; powdered components co-deposited by plasma spray
column5: The three values are pre-spray feedstock component mass fractions, not measured post-spray phase fractions. The bonded-carbide component is named nickel-bonded chromium carbide, but the patent table does not resolve the binder fraction or alloy chemistry inside that component. Powder lot, particle-size distribution, spray vendor, gun, atmosphere, standoff, power, substrate preparation, porosity, and post-spray assay are absent. NASA-CR-195445 explicitly identifies its PS-200 through this patent but does not independently assay the engine coating. Publication access is not a patent-practice or trademark license

column4: NASA-CR-195445 Wear Characteristics and Appendix A-3b; Test 3 used a stock iron rotor, stock tungsten-carbide OMC trochoid housing, and PS-200/TBC accessory and drive end side housings; the narrative reports a completed 2.5 h run
column5: The report describes the engine coating as plasma-sprayed PS-200 over zirconia and elsewhere labels the underlayer SX-331 without proving that the names are chemically identical. Aluminum alloy, temper, casting route, coating thickness, spray parameters, surface-finish method, profilometer cutoff, trace direction, repeat count, dispersion, and measurement uncertainty are absent. The narrative reports 2.5 h while the Figure 7 discussion mentions before and after a 2 h test, so the retained duration is narrative context rather than a metrology timestamp. A drive-side TBC crack attributed to a probable spray defect led the team to scrap that housing. No measurable wear is reported without a detection limit, so no zero-wear or wear-rate claim is admitted

column4: NASA-CR-195445 Wear Characteristics and Appendix A-6b; Test 6 used an OMC-style titanium rotor with alumina microspheres on its face and Cr2O3 on its sides, plus PS-200/TBC sidewalls and trochoid; the intended 2 h run was stopped at 1.5 h
column5: The report identifies the trochoid as aluminum-alloy context by stopping when its exhaust-port temperature exceeded 500 degF near the alloy's tolerable limit, but does not report alloy, temper, casting route, or lot. Plasma Technologies used a computerized cam follower after APS Materials produced an uneven first housing; the tested housing still had uneven spray and honing, local PS-200 breakthrough to zirconia, leakage paths, hot spots, and minor apex-seal roughness. Coating thickness, as-tested profile, surface-finish method, profilometer cutoff, trace direction, repeat count, dispersion, and uncertainty are absent. The two RMS values do not establish a wear rate, friction coefficient, service life, or universal Wankel coating behavior
